sources.title: Practically-A-Book Review: Rootclaim $100,000 Lab Leak Debate
sources.author: Scott Alexander
sources.role: core case material — the central synthesis of the debate; leans zoonosis

sources.title: A Bayesian analysis of the COVID origins debate
sources.author: Eric Stansifer (debate judge; applied mathematician, PhD MIT)
sources.role: a debate judge's own written Bayesian reasoning toward zoonosis (his decision itself is video/PDF-only)

sources.title: Response to Michael Weissman re. covid origins debate
sources.author: Eric Stansifer (debate judge)
sources.role: direct rebuttal of Weissman's lab-leak Bayesian analysis — a genuine cross-source contradiction

sources.title: An Inconvenient Probability (Bayesian analysis of COVID origins)
sources.author: Michael Weissman (physicist, UIUC)
sources.role: rigorous independent Bayesian analysis reaching ~300:1 for lab leak — the opposite pole to Stansifer

sources.title: COVID origins debate: Response to Scott Alexander
sources.author: Saar Wilf / Rootclaim
sources.role: the losing side's rebuttal — key for representing disagreement faithfully

claims.text: It is epidemiologically implausible for COVID to have circulated significantly before the officially detected case, given its known doubling rate

attestations.verbatim_span: it seems epidemiologically impossible for COVID to have been circulating much before the first cases were officially detected December 11. The COVID pandemic doubles every 3.5 days.
Peter argued that COVID couldn’t have been more than a few weeks old when the first wet market cases were detected. But this was based on its known doubling rate.

claims.text: About 1/10,000 of Wuhan citizens was a wet market vendor

attestations.verbatim_span: About 1/10,000 citizens of Wuhan was a wet market vendor.
1/10,000 Wuhan citizens work at the wet market

claims.text: Virologists privately said COVID could have come from a lab leak with odds of 50-50

attestations.verbatim_span: Back in the earliest stage of the pandemic, they said to each other that it seemed like COVID could have come from a lab leak - their specific odds were 50-50
There were all those virologists giving 50-50 odds in their internal conversations.
attestations.context: Argument that lab leak wasn't pseudoscience

claims.text: One should choose the desired confidence level for one's application rather than blindly following the traditional 5% error rate convention

attestations.verbatim_span: Obviously you should choose the desired confidence level for your application, and not blindly follow the traditional choice of 5% error rate, but for purpose of our discussion it is fine.
Obviously you should choose the desired confidence level for your application, and not blindly follow the traditional choice of 5% error rate

claims.text: The equivalence between conditional probability and p-value holds only for a binary observation; more generally the relationship depends on the definitions chosen for a particular application

attestations.verbatim_span: I should clarify that this is only for a binary observation
attestations.context: Clarification following disagreement from Michael Weissman

attestations.verbatim_span: Following a conversation with Michael Weissman in which he disagreed with this statement, I should clarify that this is only for a binary observation. More generally one should say that the conditional probability is related to the p-value, with the nature of that relationship depending on the definitions chosen for a particular application
attestations.context: Footnote correction based on external feedback

claims.text: Using the two-tailed sorting method, an observation of 70 heads (i.e., the total probability of seeing 0-29 or 71-100 heads under the null hypothesis) gets a p-value of 0.00006

attestations.verbatim_span: Now if we observe 70 heads, this has a higher p-value than any observation of 0 to 29 or 71 to 100 heads, but a lower p-value than 31 to 69 heads; therefore it gets a p-value of 0.00006
The total probability of seeing 0 to 29 or 71 to 100 heads, conditioned on , is 0.00006.

claims.text: Wuhan has less than 1% of China's population (about 0.7%).

attestations.verbatim_span: Wuhan has less than 1% of China’s population.
About 0.7% of the population of China lives in Wuhan.

claims.text: A Bayes factor (likelihood ratio) is the ratio of conditional probabilities under different hypotheses, and this ratio measures the strength of evidence.

attestations.verbatim_span: There is no contention that the strength of evidence is measured by the ratio of the conditional probabilities of that evidence under the different hypotheses – how likely are we to encounter such evidence assuming a certain hypothesis is true (Known as the Bayes factor or likelihood ratio )
A Bayes factor is the ratio of conditional probabilities

claims.text: An empirical analysis reveals that seafood markets and facilities repeatedly formed initial Covid clusters following periods of zero infections.

attestations.verbatim_span: There are multiple cases where a country has had zero Covid cases for a while, and then a cluster of cases appears in a seafood market. In all these outbreaks, there is no contention that the source is not zoonotic, as it is genetically descended from the Wuhan outbreak
Contrary to this claim, an empirical analysis reveals that seafood markets and facilities repeatedly formed initial Covid clusters following a period of zero infections.

claims.text: This pattern was observed in 2 out of 5 large outbreaks in China in 2020 (Xinfadi and Dalian), as well as outbreaks in Thailand and Singapore.

attestations.verbatim_span: 2 out of 5 large outbreaks in China in 2020, as well as outbreaks in Thailand and Singapore, started in markets
This was observed in 2 out of 5 large outbreaks in China in 2020 ( Xinfadi and Dalian ), as well as in outbreaks in Thailand and Singapore

claims.text: Three independent methods all support a conditional probability estimate of over 1% for HSM forming an early cluster.

attestations.verbatim_span: We use three independent methods to estimate this probability more accurately, all pointing to this number being larger than 1%
We provide three methods to estimate this number, all of them supporting a conclusion that this conditional probability is over 1%.

claims.text: Combining the market-share and HSM-share factors yields a conditional probability of HSM spillover of around 3-5%.

attestations.verbatim_span: we calculate (below) a conditional probability of 3-5%
Multiplying the share of markets (1 and 2) by the share of HSM within markets (3), we converge to around 3-5%.
attestations.context: Result of conditional probability calculation for p(HSM|Zoonosis, Wuhan)

claims.text: It is possible to reach high, even smoking-gun levels of confidence through good inference by examining multiple pieces of evidence, even when a single smoking-gun piece of evidence is rare, demonstrating that good inference is superior to relying on singular strong evidence.

attestations.verbatim_span: Nevertheless, it is possible to reach high confidence when examining multiple pieces of evidence, which is another reason why having good inference is superior to having good evidence.
While smoking-gun evidence is hard to come by, reaching smoking-gun levels of confidence is possible through good inference.

claims.text: Scott's calculation assumes zoonosis will almost always start in markets, implying nearly all spillovers happen in wildlife markets.

attestations.verbatim_span: The 25% number is equivalent to claiming that nearly all zoonosis spillovers happen in markets with wildlife
His calculation assumes zoonosis will almost always start in markets

claims.text: Scott gives no additional/zero weight to the specific conditions present in HSM.

attestations.verbatim_span: He then gives no weight at all to the conditions in HSM
attestations.context: Critique of Scott's treatment of HSM

attestations.verbatim_span: He then gives no weight at all (Zero!) to the conditions in HSM
attestations.framing: Zero!

claims.text: Rootclaim structured a debate that rewards superior memorization rather than reasoning quality, giving a major advantage to the debater with more memorized knowledge of the issue

attestations.verbatim_span: the debate structure provided a major advantage to the debater with more memorized knowledge of the issue
we structured a debate that rewards it

claims.text: Stansifer's probability estimate (0.02) for a researcher working on a SARS2-like virus for weeks or months under BSL-2 getting infected was a severe underestimate and highly overconfident

attestations.verbatim_span: Severe underestimate (0.02) of the probability that a researcher working on a SARS2-like virus for weeks or months under BSL-2 would get infected.
attestations.framing: Labeled as 'severe underestimate'

attestations.verbatim_span: but 2% is highly overconfident
attestations.framing: Loaded term 'highly overconfident' applied to opposing estimate

claims.text: The zoonosis side made at least as many factual mistakes during the debate as the lab-leak (Rootclaim) side did

attestations.verbatim_span: Also worth noting that the zoonosis side has made at least as many factual mistakes during the debate
attestations.context: Jonathan's comparative claim about factual errors

attestations.verbatim_span: Also worth noting that the zoonosis side has made at least as many factual mistakes during the debate…
attestations.context: Quoted Rootclaim statement, cited by Chris

claims.text: It is incorrect to claim an HSM worker is as likely as any random Wuhan resident to be the index case under the lab leak hypothesis

attestations.verbatim_span: It is clearly wrong to claim that an HSM worker is as likely as any Wuhan resident to be the index case under lab leak
attestations.context: Jonathan's critique of judges' specific error #1

attestations.verbatim_span: It is clearly wrong to claim that an HSM worker is as likely as any Wuhan resident to be the index case under lab leak,
attestations.context: Tal (Rootclaim) reply, agreeing with a stated proposition

claims.text: The judges accepted (or the random-location model implies) an extremely low likelihood (p=0.0001, i.e., roughly 1-in-10,000) for HSM to form an early cluster of infected patients under lab leak

attestations.verbatim_span: The first mistake in the judges’ decision was accepting an extremely low likelihood for the Huanan Seafood Market (HSM) to form an early cluster of infected patients if Covid originated in a lab.
Consider HSM to be a random location in Wuhan (i.e. will form an early cluster only once every 10,000 hypothetical SARS2 lab leaks in Wuhan).
attestations.context: First commenter critiquing Rootclaim's probability model

claims.text: The judges did not make any significant mistakes favoring lab-leak (Rootclaim's side) that need to be corrected

attestations.verbatim_span: the judges didn’t make any significant mistakes favoring lab-leak
so there are no mistakes favoring our side to remove

claims.text: In the real world, having a robust inference method is far more important than the facts/raw evidence quantity themselves

attestations.verbatim_span: It is just that, in the real world, having a robust inference method is far more important.
While the first is true

claims.text: Saar Wilf has been developing a new reasoning method since 2016

attestations.verbatim_span: Since 2016, he’s been developing a new form of reasoning, meant to transcend normal human bias.
attestations.context: Introduction of Saar Wilf and Rootclaim

claims.text: Rootclaim uses Bayesian reasoning

attestations.verbatim_span: His method - called Rootclaim - uses Bayesian reasoning, a branch of math that explains the right way to weigh evidence.

claims.text: Bayesian reasoning is widely supported among statisticians and reasoning experts

attestations.verbatim_span: Everyone supports Bayesian reasoning. The statisticians support it, I support it, Nate Silver wrote a whole book supporting it.
attestations.context: Discussion of Bayesian reasoning's general acceptance

claims.text: Most practitioners do not attempt full formal Bayesian reasoning on complex real-world problems, relying instead on intuition

attestations.verbatim_span: Nobody - not the statisticians, not Nate Silver, certainly not me - tries to do full Bayesian reasoning on fuzzy real-world problems.
attestations.context: Author's commentary on practical limits of Bayesian reasoning

claims.text: Attempting full Bayesian modeling on fuzzy problems risks philosophical errors that could make outcomes worse than intuitive reasoning

attestations.verbatim_span: You’d make some philosophical mistake converting the situation into numbers, then end up much worse off than if you’d tried normal human intuition.
attestations.context: Author's skepticism about formal Bayesian modeling

claims.text: Rootclaim calculated an 86% chance that Putin does not have cancer

attestations.verbatim_span: we end up with the final calculation: 86% chance Putin doesn’t have cancer, too bad.
attestations.context: Example of Rootclaim's Bayesian analysis methodology

claims.text: Saar used Rootclaim to determine that the main suspect in an Israeli murder case was likely innocent and a local mental patient committed the crime

attestations.verbatim_span: Saar used Rootclaim to determine that the main suspect was likely innocent, and a local mental patient had committed the crime
attestations.context: Example of past Rootclaim analysis success

claims.text: New DNA evidence later appeared to support Rootclaim's conclusion in the murder case

attestations.verbatim_span: later, new DNA evidence seemed to back him up.
attestations.context: Follow-up on Israeli murder case example

claims.text: Saar Wilf sold his fraud detection startup to PayPal for $169 million in 2008

attestations.verbatim_span: In 2008, he sold his fraud detection startup to PayPal for $169 million.

claims.text: Saar issued a $100,000 challenge to anyone who disagreed with Rootclaim's analyses

attestations.verbatim_span: If you disagree with any of his Rootclaim analyses - you think Putin does have cancer, or whatever - he and the Rootclaim team will bet you $100,000 that they’re right.

claims.text: Rootclaim found in favor of the lab leak hypothesis of COVID

attestations.verbatim_span: Rootclaim also found in favor of the lab leak hypothesis of COVID.
attestations.context: Introduction to the specific debate topic

claims.text: Peter Miller agreed to take Saar's $100K bet regarding the lab leak hypothesis

attestations.verbatim_span: fellow commenter tgof137 (Peter Miller) agreed to take him up on his $100K bet.

claims.text: Peter Miller became 90%+ convinced the lab leak hypothesis was false after investigating

attestations.verbatim_span: he ended up “90%+” convinced it was false
attestations.context: Peter's background and shift in views

claims.text: Peter believed there was no incentive to defend the zoonosis hypothesis while contrarian bloggers profited from promoting lab leak

attestations.verbatim_span: contrarian bloggers were raking in Substack cash by promoting lab leak, but there seemed to be no incentive to defend zoonosis.
attestations.context: Peter's motivation for taking the bet

claims.text: Peter expected an unbiased judge would favor zoonosis with about 80% odds after seeing the evidence

attestations.verbatim_span: I [expected] a smart and unbiased person would vote for zoonosis with, say, 80% odds after seeing all the evidence.
attestations.context: Peter's email explaining his risk calculation

claims.text: Peter calculated the odds of losing to both judges as approximately 20% squared, assuming independence

attestations.verbatim_span: If both judges voting for lab origin is uncorrelated, that's 20% squared, and it was pretty low odds of a catastrophic financial risk for me.

claims.text: Peter was confident in his scientific correctness but worried about performance anxiety at the debate

attestations.verbatim_span: I wasn't highly worried about losing the debate because I was wrong about the science. I put in enough effort to know I'm probably correct there. My biggest fear was that I'd choke at the debate for some reason
attestations.context: Peter's stated concerns before the debate

claims.text: Peter compared the debate's stakes to mountain climbing risk, describing a 5% chance of death in climbing as more significant than debate stakes

attestations.verbatim_span: Peter told blogger Philipp Markolin that he “is a mountain climber where sometimes there is a 5% chance to die, and the stakes are just not that high for a debate.”

claims.text: The debate had two judges: Will van Treuren and Eric Stansifer

attestations.verbatim_span:
Will van Treuren, a pharmaceutical entrepreneur with a PhD from Stanford and a background in bacteriology and immunology.
Eric Stansifer, an applied mathematician with a PhD from MIT and experience in mathematical virology.

claims.text: The first officially confirmed COVID case was a vendor at the Wuhan wet market

attestations.verbatim_span: The first officially confirmed COVID case was a vendor at the Wuhan wet market.

claims.text: The next four confirmed cases and half of the next 40 were also linked to the wet market

attestations.verbatim_span: So were the next four, and half of the next 40.

claims.text: A heat map of early COVID cases is centered on the wet market rather than the WIV lab

attestations.verbatim_span: A heat map of early cases is obviously centered on the wet market, not on the lab.

claims.text: The wet market and the WIV lab are about 6 miles apart by straight line, or a 15 mile/half hour drive

attestations.verbatim_span: The wet market and the lab are about 6 miles away as the crow flies, or a 15 mile / half hour drive.

claims.text: A map of cases within the wet market shows a pattern concentrated in the southwest corner

attestations.verbatim_span: A map of cases at the wet market itself shows a clear pattern in favor of the very southwest corner

claims.text: The southwest corner of the wet market is where most wildlife was sold, including rumored raccoon-dog stalls

attestations.verbatim_span: The southwest corner is where most of the wildlife was being sold. Rumor said that included a stall with raccoon-dogs, an animal which is generally teeming with weird coronaviruses, and is a plausible intermediate host between humans and bats
attestations.context: Peter's argument for zoonotic origin via raccoon-dogs

claims.text: China denied the raccoon-dog stall rumor and refused to release information

attestations.verbatim_span: China said this rumor was false and refused to release any information.

claims.text: A virologist's 2014 photo confirmed the existence of a raccoon-dog stall at the market

attestations.verbatim_span: a virologist had visited Wuhan in 2014, saw the awful conditions in the shop, and took a picture as an example of the kind of place that a future pandemic might start.
attestations.context: Evidence supporting existence of raccoon-dog stall

claims.text: It would be a surprising coincidence for a zoonotic pandemic to start in a city with a major coronavirus lab

attestations.verbatim_span: People say it would be a surprising coincidence if a zoonotic coronavirus pandemic just so happened to start in a city with a big coronavirus research lab, and this is true.
attestations.context: Author's framing of competing coincidence arguments

claims.text: It would be an even more surprising coincidence for a lab leak to be first detected specifically at a raccoon-dog stall in a wet market

attestations.verbatim_span: it would be an even more surprising coincidence if a lab-leak coronavirus pandemic just so happened to first get detected at a raccoon-dog stall in a wet market!
attestations.framing: favors zoonosis by emphasizing coincidence burden on lab leak hypothesis

claims.text: It is not clear that the first COVID case was at the wet market; a Mr. Chen with no market connection fell sick December 8

attestations.verbatim_span: It’s not clear that the first case was at the wet market; a certain Mr. Chen, with no connection to the market, seems to have fallen sick on December 8.

claims.text: An SCMP article suggested there were 92 previously undetected cases suspicious for COVID as far back as November

attestations.verbatim_span: An SCMP article suggested there were 92 previously-undetected cases suspicious for COVID as far back as November.

claims.text: Half of the first forty universally-agreed-upon cases lacked market connections

attestations.verbatim_span: even if half of the first forty universally-agreed-upon cases had market connections that means another half didn’t.

claims.text: There was a detection bias toward identifying cases with market connections because authorities suspected the market as the origin

attestations.verbatim_span: There was a bias towards detecting cases at the market: because authorities thought the market was the origin, and because everyone was thinking about zoonosis after SARS1, they only screened/diagnosed people with a market connection.

claims.text: A non-market-connected COVID case was only detected because a hospital worker recognized symptoms in a relative

attestations.verbatim_span: One of the few non-market-connected COVID cases detected during this period was only detected because he was the relative of a hospital worker; the worker noticed the signs and insisted they go to the hospital despite the lack of a wet market connection.
attestations.context: Saar's supporting anecdote for detection bias

claims.text: The concentration of positive samples near the raccoon-dog stall could be due to sampling bias rather than true case concentration

attestations.verbatim_span: that could be because that area was sampled more; it’s also close to the mahjong room, where visitors and vendors at the market would go and unwind in a tight, poorly ventilated area.
attestations.context: Saar's alternative explanation for market case clustering

claims.text: WIV was conducting substantial gain-of-function research

attestations.verbatim_span: they were doing lots of gain of function research.

claims.text: A proposed lab leak scenario: a WIV worker was infected with a modified coronavirus and spread it to contacts before the market outbreak

attestations.verbatim_span: one story compatible with the evidence is that a worker at WIV got infected with their modified coronavirus and passed it to his contacts.

claims.text: China suppressed evidence of lab research related to COVID, as in previous lab leak cases such as the USSR

attestations.verbatim_span: China quashed all evidence of the lab research (as was done in previous lab leak cases, eg the USSR) so all we have is the apparent wet market links that Peter found so convincing.
attestations.context: Saar's explanation for lack of direct lab leak evidence

claims.text: The supposed pre-wet-market cases identified by WHO were confirmed not to be COVID after testing

attestations.verbatim_span: their final investigation, which included testing samples from these people after good tests became available, found that none of these people really had COVID.
attestations.context: Peter's rebuttal regarding the 92 WHO-identified cases

claims.text: Mr. Chen was hospitalized for dental issues on December 8 and caught COVID in the hospital on December 16, after the first wet market cases

attestations.verbatim_span: he said in an interview that he was hospitalized for dental issues on December 8, caught COVID in the hospital on December 16, and then was erroneously reported as “hospitalized for COVID on December 8”.
attestations.context: Peter's rebuttal on Mr. Chen's case

claims.text: If the first infection occurred around November 11, roughly 256 times as many cases would be expected than were observed

attestations.verbatim_span: if the first infection was much earlier - let’s say November 11 - we would expect 256x as much COVID as we actually saw.

claims.text: Observed hospitalization and death counts in January were consistent with a pandemic start in early December, not with a much earlier start

attestations.verbatim_span: the number of hospitalizations, deaths, etc, in January or whenever were all consistent with the number of people you’d expect if the pandemic started in early December - and not consistent with 256x that many people.

claims.text: The first reported case, a wet market vendor on December 11, was likely very early in the pandemic though not literally the first case

attestations.verbatim_span: So probably we should just accept that the first reported case - a wet market vendor, December 11 - was very early in the pandemic. She wasn’t literally the first case - that would most likely have been someone who worked at the raccoon-dog shop

claims.text: Most COVID cases (about 95%) are mild enough not to require medical attention

attestations.verbatim_span: whose case might (like 95% of COVID cases) have been mild enough not to come to medical attention.

claims.text: COVID was suspected to spread through a wet market and Chinese authorities started deliberately looking for wet market connections only on December 30

attestations.verbatim_span: started deliberately looking for wet market connections, this only happened on December 30
attestations.context: discussion of bias in early case ascertainment

claims.text: The earliest cases, including the 40 very earliest cases where half came from the wet market, were not biased by the later search for wet market connections

attestations.verbatim_span: So the earliest cases - including the 40 very earliest cases where half came from the wet market - weren’t biased (at least not through that particular route)
attestations.context: discussion of bias in early case ascertainment

claims.text: The claim that the first case and half of the first 40 cases had wet market connections is real and convincing evidence

attestations.verbatim_span: So the claim that “the first case, and half of the first 40 cases, had wet market connections” stands as real and convincing evidence.

claims.text: The exact center of the map of positive COVID samples in the wet market was the mahjong room

attestations.verbatim_span: Although the exact center of the map of positive COVID samples in the wet market was the mahjong room

claims.text: The samples taken from the mahjong room itself were not positive

attestations.verbatim_span: the samples taken from the mahjong room were not, themselves, positive

claims.text: There was no real super-spreader event at the wet market

attestations.verbatim_span: There was no real “super-spreader event” at the wet market.
attestations.context: discussion of spread pattern at wet market

claims.text: The spread pattern at the wet market was a slow burn, with one case the first day and progressively more over subsequent days

attestations.verbatim_span: There was a slow burn - one case the first day, a few more the next day, a few more the day after that.
attestations.context: discussion of spread pattern at wet market

claims.text: It is hard to see how a single visit from an infected lab worker could produce this slow-burn spread pattern

attestations.verbatim_span: It’s hard to see how a single visit from an infected lab worker could do that.
attestations.context: discussion of spread pattern at wet market

claims.text: For the lab leak hypothesis to explain the wet market data, the lab would have to have leaked in late November, infected exactly one lab worker, who went to the wet market, infected a vendor, then went home and quarantined, with all other cases downstream of that vendor

attestations.verbatim_span: So the only way it could possibly be a lab leak is if the lab leaked sometime in late November, infected exactly one lab worker, that worker went straight to the wet market, infected a vendor, then went home, quarantined, recovered, and all other cases were downstream of that first infected wet market vendor.
attestations.context: discussion of lab leak scenario needed to fit wet market evidence

claims.text: This specific lab leak scenario is unparsimonious

attestations.verbatim_span: This is unparsimonious.
attestations.context: discussion of lab leak scenario needed to fit wet market evidence

claims.text: The only source saying Mr. Chen got sick early was an anonymous interview

attestations.verbatim_span: The only source saying that Mr. Chen got sick early was an anonymous interview.
attestations.context: Saar's argument about index case timing

claims.text: Nobody was able to find any wet market connections for Mr. Chen even if he got sick later than the first wet market cases

attestations.verbatim_span: And even if he was later than the first wet market cases, nobody was able to find any wet market connections.
attestations.context: Saar's argument about index case timing

claims.text: Whoever infected Mr. Chen was earlier than the index case and not linked to the wet market

attestations.verbatim_span: This means that whoever infected him was earlier than the index case and not linked to the wet market.
attestations.context: Saar's argument about index case timing

claims.text: If pre-discovery COVID had a slower doubling time than known COVID, it could have been circulating longer than Peter's estimate

attestations.verbatim_span: If pre-discovery COVID had a slower doubling time than known COVID, it could have been around longer.

claims.text: Post-lockdown serology suggested case numbers larger than claimed at the time

attestations.verbatim_span: And post-lockdown serology suggested numbers that were larger than claimed at the time.

claims.text: The infection could have been circulating for weeks rather than requiring the first lab worker to go straight to the market

attestations.verbatim_span: So contra Peter’s claims, the infection could have been going on longer, which wouldn’t require the first lab worker to go straight to the market. It could have been weeks.

claims.text: Dr. Jesse Bloom's investigation of wet market samples, considered the most conclusive, failed to find a clear connection between COVID and raccoon-dogs or other animals

attestations.verbatim_span: Dr. Jesse Bloom’s investigation of the wet market samples, considered the final and most conclusive, failed to find a clear connection between COVID and raccoon-dogs or any other animals.

claims.text: A formal statistical analysis of which animals' DNA was found near COVID samples most often placed raccoon dogs near the bottom

attestations.verbatim_span: if you do a formal statistical analysis of which animals’ DNA was found near COVID samples most often, raccoon dogs are near the bottom.

claims.text: Wide-mouth bass, which cannot get COVID, topped the statistical analysis of animal DNA co-occurrence with COVID samples

attestations.verbatim_span: The top is wide-mouth bass, which can’t get COVID.

claims.text: The bass result is obviously contamination, probably from infected humans touching wide-mouth bass tanks

attestations.verbatim_span: This is obviously contamination, probably from infected humans touching wide-mouth bass tanks or something.

claims.text: The Chinese data included a negative sample from a mahjong table but also mentioned poultry being sold nearby, suggesting it might not be the mahjong room itself

attestations.verbatim_span: Although the Chinese data included a negative sample from a mahjong table, it included a mention of poultry being sold nearby, which might mean this wasn’t the mahjong room itself, but some other mahjong table at a poultry shop elsewhere in the market
attestations.context: discussion of mahjong sample location ambiguity

claims.text: Dry mahjong tables might not hold the virus well

attestations.verbatim_span: (dry) mahjong tables might not hold the virus well anyway
attestations.context: discussion of mahjong sample location ambiguity

claims.text: Raccoon-dogs were sold in various cages at various stalls, separated by air gaps large enough to present a challenge for COVID transmission

attestations.verbatim_span: Peter: Raccoon-dogs were sold in various cages at various stalls, separated by air gaps big enough to present a challenge for COVID transmission

claims.text: There is no reason to think one raccoon-dog would automatically pass COVID to all others

attestations.verbatim_span: there’s no reason to think that one raccoon-dog would automatically pass it to all the others.

claims.text: The statistical analysis only proves there were many raccoon-dogs without COVID, but only one infected raccoon-dog is needed for the hypothesis

attestations.verbatim_span: The statistical analysis just proves there were many raccoon-dogs who didn’t have COVID. But you only need one.

claims.text: The raccoon dog shop and the drain leading out of it had some of the highest positive sample rates

attestations.verbatim_span: The raccoon dog shop and the drain leading out of the raccoon dog shop had some of the highest positive sample rates, which is more interesting than a statistical analysis which everyone agrees must be wrong (since it favors bass).

claims.text: The statistical analysis favoring bass is agreed by everyone to be wrong

attestations.verbatim_span: a statistical analysis which everyone agrees must be wrong (since it favors bass)

claims.text: Based on the stated location, the negative mahjong sample is definitely from the mahjong room, despite the unclear poultry connection

attestations.verbatim_span: It’s unclear why the negative mahjong sample says something about poultry, but based on the stated location, it’s definitely the one in the mahjong room.

claims.text: The coronavirus eventually mutated into many different strains, with the first big split seen in early samples being between Lineage A and Lineage B, differing by two mutations

attestations.verbatim_span: Yuri: The coronavirus eventually mutated into many different strains. But the first big split, seen in some of the earliest samples, is between two different sub-strains called Lineage A and Lineage B, which differ by two mutations.

claims.text: In the two distinguishing mutations, Lineage A matches BANAL-52 (the closest known bat virus relative of COVID), while Lineage B differs

attestations.verbatim_span: In these two mutations, Lineage A is the same as BANAL-52, a bat virus which is the closest-known relative of COVID, but Lineage B is different.

claims.text: Since COVID probably evolved from something like BANAL-52, Lineage A must have come first, spread for a while, and then acquired two new mutations turning it into Lineage B

attestations.verbatim_span: Since COVID probably evolved from something like BANAL-52, Lineage A must have come first, spread for a while, and then gotten two new mutations, turning it into Lineage B.

claims.text: All cases at the wet market, including the first detected case, were Lineage B

attestations.verbatim_span: All of the cases at the wet market, including the first detected case, were Lineage B.

claims.text: Lineage A wasn't discovered until about a week after Lineage B cases, and none of the Lineage A patients had been to the wet market

attestations.verbatim_span: Lineage A wasn’t discovered until about a week later, and none of the Lineage A patients had been to the wet market.

claims.text: This lineage pattern matches Saar's story that the lab leaked to somewhere else in Wuhan, the virus spread undetected and mutated to Lineage B, then someone with Lineage B went to the wet market and started a superspreader event, with Lineage A cases detected later

attestations.verbatim_span: This matches Saar’s story above. The lab leaked to somewhere else in Wuhan, not the wet market. The virus spread undetected in the population for a while. During this time, it mutated to Lineage B. Then one of the people with Lineage B went to the wet market and started a superspreader event. The authorities sampled the patients, found Lineage B, then started looking elsewhere. Later they detected some of the earlier Lineage A cases.

claims.text: The market is unlikely to be the origin of the pandemic because the original Lineage A strain wasn't found there

attestations.verbatim_span: The market is unlikely to be the origin of the pandemic, because the original Lineage A strain wasn’t found there.

claims.text: Although Lineage A is evolutionarily older, Lineage B started spreading in humans first

attestations.verbatim_span: Peter: Although Lineage A is evolutionarily older, Lineage B started spreading in humans first.

claims.text: Throughout the early pandemic, until D614G drove other strains extinct, a consistent 2/3 of cases were Lineage B compared to 1/3 Lineage A

attestations.verbatim_span: Throughout the early pandemic, until the D614G variant drove all other strains extinct, a consistent 2/3 of the cases were B, compared to 1/3 A.

claims.text: Both lineages spread at the same rate, so the best explanation for the 2:1 ratio is that Lineage B started earlier than Lineage A

attestations.verbatim_span: Both strains spread at the same rate, so the best explanation is that B started earlier than A.

claims.text: Since COVID doubles every 3-4 days, Lineage B probably started 3-4 days earlier than Lineage A, explaining the consistent twice-as-many case ratio

attestations.verbatim_span: Since COVID doubles every 3-4 days, probably Lineage B started 3-4 days earlier than Lineage A, which explains why it’s always been twice as many cases.

claims.text: Lineage B has more internal genetic diversity than Lineage A

attestations.verbatim_span: But also, Lineage B also has more internal genetic diversity than Lineage A.

claims.text: In general, older viruses have more genetic diversity (the molecular clock principle)

attestations.verbatim_span: In general, older viruses have more genetic diversity (the “molecular clock”).

claims.text: The greater genetic diversity of Lineage B is further evidence that it started spreading first

attestations.verbatim_span: This is further evidence that B started spreading first.

claims.text: Pekar 2022 and Pipes 2021 analyses using known parameters for spread rate and diversity find 90%+ odds that Lineage B was the first one in humans

attestations.verbatim_span: Pekar 2022 and Pipes 2021 do analyses with known parameters for spread rate and diversity, and find 90%+ odds that Lineage B was the first one in humans.

claims.text: The virus probably crossed from bats into raccoon-dogs on a raccoon-dog farm in the countryside, then spread among raccoon-dogs accumulating mutations forming both lineages

attestations.verbatim_span: Probably the virus crossed from bats into raccoon-dogs on some raccoon-dog farm out in the country. It spread in the raccoon-dogs for a while, racking up mutations, including the (less mutated) Lineage A strain and the (slightly more mutated) Lineage B strain.

claims.text: Several raccoon-dogs carrying Lineage A and Lineage B were taken to Wuhan for sale, and the Lineage B one passed its virus to humans earlier, followed 3-4 days later by the Lineage A one

attestations.verbatim_span: Then several raccoon-dogs were taken to Wuhan for sale, including one with Lineage A and another with Lineage B. The one with Lineage B passed its virus to humans earlier. Then 3-4 days later, the Lineage A one passed its virus to humans.

claims.text: Lineage A was first found in a Wuhan neighborhood closer to the wet market than 97% of Wuhan's population

attestations.verbatim_span: Lineage A was first found in a Wuhan neighborhood right next to the wet market (closer to the wet market than 97% of Wuhan’s population).

claims.text: It would be a bizarre coincidence if a lab leak pandemic separated into two strains and both were first detected at a wet market

attestations.verbatim_span: But it would be an even more bizarre coincidence if a lab leak pandemic separated into two strains, and both were first detected at a wet market!
attestations.framing: loaded rhetorical framing emphasizing improbability of lab leak

claims.text: A positive Lineage A environmental sample was found at the wet market even though no known wet market cases were Lineage A

attestations.verbatim_span: Although no known wet market cases were Lineage A, a positive Lineage A environmental sample was found at the wet market, and everyone agrees most cases went undetected.

claims.text: A possible explanation is that the Lineage B raccoon-dog spread virus to a vendor and stayed mostly in the market, while the Lineage A raccoon-dog spread virus to a customer who carried it to nearby neighborhoods

attestations.verbatim_span: So maybe the Lineage B raccoon-dog spread its virus to a vendor, and that sub-strain mostly stayed in the market. But the Lineage A raccoon-dog spread its virus to a customer, who went back to his house nearby, and that strain spread in the neighborhoods next to the market.

claims.text: The double-spillover story is the only one that explains the evolutionary precedence of Lineage A, the greater spread and older molecular clock of Lineage B, and the fact that both strains were first found close to the wet market

attestations.verbatim_span: This is the only story that explains the evolutionary precedence of A, the greater spread and older molecular clock of B, and the fact that both strains were first found very close to the wet market.

claims.text: Lineage B could be more common and diverse because it benefited from a super-spreader event in the wet market

attestations.verbatim_span: Yuri/Saar: Lineage B could be more common and diverse because it got the advantage of a super-spreader event in the wet market.
attestations.context: counter-argument to Peter's lineage timing claim

claims.text: There are scattered cases of intermediates between Lineage A and B, and other scattered cases of lineages seeming even more ancestral than either

attestations.verbatim_span: There are a few scattered cases of intermediates between A and B, and a few other scattered cases of lineages that seem even more ancestral (ie closer to the bat virus) than either.

claims.text: The existence of intermediate/progenitor lineages doesn't make sense under the double spillover hypothesis, but does make sense if lineages separated during human transmission between the lab and the first superspreader event

attestations.verbatim_span: This doesn’t make sense in a double spillover hypothesis. But it does make sense if the lineages separated in human transmission somewhere between the lab and the first super-spreader event at the wet market.

claims.text: The wet market was not a super-spreader event; COVID spread there at exactly its normal spread rate, doubling about once every 3.5 days

attestations.verbatim_span: Peter: Again, the wet market wasn’t a super-spreader event. COVID spread in the wet market at exactly its normal spread rate, doubling about once every 3.5 days.

claims.text: The scattered cases of intermediates are sequencing errors caused by autofill software

attestations.verbatim_span: The scattered cases of “intermediates” are sequencing errors. They were all found by the same computer software, which “autofills” unsequenced bases in a genome to the most plausible guess.

claims.text: Because Lineage B was already known to the software, sequencing partial Lineage A viruses could result in autofilled sections appearing as Lineage B, creating apparent intermediates

attestations.verbatim_span: Because Lineage B was already in the software, depending on which part of a Lineage A virus you sequenced, you might get one half or the other autofilled as Lineage B, which looked like an “intermediate”.

claims.text: All the supposed intermediates were partial cases sequenced by this particular software

attestations.verbatim_span: We know this because all the supposed “intermediates” were partial cases sequenced by this particular software.

claims.text: There are too many intermediates for them to be real, since both (T/T) and (C/C) intermediate forms were found, but only one could be a real intermediate

attestations.verbatim_span: We can confirm this by noting that there are too many intermediates! That is, where Lineage A is (T/C) and Lineage B is (C/T), the software found both (T/T) “intermediates” and (C/C) “intermediates”. But obviously there can only be one real intermediate form, and we have to dismiss one or the other.

claims.text: Both apparent intermediate forms can be dismissed because they were both caused by the same software bug

attestations.verbatim_span: But in fact we can dismiss both, because they were both caused by the same software bug.

claims.text: The scattered progenitor cases are reversions, where a new mutation happened to hit an already-mutated base and shift it back toward the ancestral virus

attestations.verbatim_span: The scattered “progenitor” cases - those closer to the ancestral bat virus than either A or B - are reversions, ie cases where a new mutation in the virus happened to hit an already-mutated base and shift it back towards the ancestral virus.

claims.text: All the progenitor cases were scattered cases found months after the pandemic started, often in different countries from Wuhan

attestations.verbatim_span: We know this because all of these “progenitors” were scattered cases found months after the pandemic started, often in entirely different countries from Wuhan.

claims.text: If the progenitor cases were real progenitor viruses, they would have either fizzled out or exploded into a substantial portion of cases, not appeared as a single case in one country

attestations.verbatim_span: If these were real progenitor viruses, they would have either fizzled out or exploded into a substantial portion of all cases, not be found one time in one guy in Malaysia.

claims.text: Given the number of mutations the virus developed over the pandemic, some mutations reverting closer to the original bat virus are inevitable, and the observed number of such progenitor-appearing viruses matches statistical expectation by chance

attestations.verbatim_span: Given the number of mutations the virus developed over the course of the pandemic, it’s inevitable that some of them would be mutations that bring it closer to the original bat virus, and in fact we find the number of “progenitors” found very nicely matches the number of progenitor-appearing viruses we would expect by chance.

claims.text: In many cases, the progenitor viruses can be shown to be newer than the original lineages because they also carry some of the later mutations that Lineage A or B picked up

attestations.verbatim_span: And in many cases, we know the “progenitors” are newer than the original lineages, because they also have some of the later mutations that Lineage A or B picked up along the way, alongside their apparent ancestral-bat-virus-like mutations.

claims.text: Two years before COVID, scientists at the Wuhan Institute of Virology and University of North Carolina colleagues submitted a grant proposal for the DEFUSE program

attestations.verbatim_span: Yuri: Two years before COVID, scientists at the Wuhan Institute of Virology, together with colleagues at the University of North Carolina, sent in a grant proposal for the DEFUSE program.
attestations.context: introduction to Session 2 on viral genetics/DEFUSE

claims.text: The DEFUSE program was intended to locate and better understand potential future pandemic viruses by collecting new coronaviruses from bat caves

attestations.verbatim_span: This program, intended to locate and better understand potential future pandemic viruses, involved going into bat caves and collecting new coronaviruses.

claims.text: The DEFUSE program planned to perform gain-of-function research by adding a furin cleavage site to collected coronaviruses to make them more infectious

attestations.verbatim_span: Once they had them, they would do gain-of-function: specifically, they would add a furin cleavage site to make them more infectious and see what happened.

claims.text: COVID's spike protein has two sections, one that binds to human cells via the ACE2 receptor and another that helps fuse with the cell after binding, both hidden in one spike to avoid the immune system

attestations.verbatim_span: COVID’s spike protein has two sections: one binds to human cells through the ACE2 receptor, the other helps fuse with the cell after binding. In order to avoid the immune system, it hides both of these into one spike.
attestations.context: explanatory interlude on furin cleavage site

claims.text: COVID uses the human respiratory enzyme furin to separate its spike protein sections, which also ensures it targets human respiratory cells specifically

attestations.verbatim_span: It takes advantage of a human respiratory enzyme, furin, to do the separation - this also ensures that it only infects its primary target, human respiratory cells.
attestations.context: explanatory interlude on furin cleavage site

claims.text: The part of COVID that allows separation by furin is called the furin cleavage site

attestations.verbatim_span: The part of COVID that lets it get separated by furin is called the “furin cleavage site”.
attestations.context: explanatory interlude on furin cleavage site

claims.text: COVID's bat-virus ancestors were gastrointestinal viruses

attestations.verbatim_span: COVID’s bat-virus ancestors were gastrointestinal viruses
attestations.context: explanatory interlude on furin cleavage site

claims.text: COVID's two closest known relatives are bat viruses RATG-13 and BANAL-52

attestations.verbatim_span: We’ve found two close relatives of COVID: bat viruses called RATG-13 and BANAL-52.

claims.text: COVID resembles BANAL-52 plus a furin cleavage site

attestations.verbatim_span: COVID looks more or less like BANAL-52 plus a furin cleavage site.

claims.text: None of the 1500 known sarbecoviruses besides COVID have furin cleavage sites

attestations.verbatim_span: There are 1500 sarbecoviruses, members of the family of viruses that includes SARS and SARS2/COVID. None of them except COVID have furin cleavage sites.

claims.text: BANAL-52 lacks any sequence resembling a precursor to COVID's furin cleavage site

attestations.verbatim_span: BANAL-52, COVID’s closest ancestor, doesn’t even have anything resembling one that could mutate into a functional furin cleavage site like COVID’s.

claims.text: COVID has twelve new nucleotides forming a fully formed furin cleavage site not present in BANAL-52

attestations.verbatim_span: COVID... has twelve completely new nucleotides in a row - a fully formed furin cleavage site that came out of nowhere.
attestations.framing: the furin cleavage site 'came out of nowhere'

claims.text: The furin cleavage site region is the only place where COVID differs profoundly from BANAL-52

attestations.verbatim_span: There is nowhere else in the genome that COVID differs from BANAL-52 in such a profound way.

claims.text: The furin cleavage site encodes arginine twice using the codon CGG, which is typical of higher animals rather than viruses

attestations.verbatim_span: But instead of the nucleotides coding for arginine in the usual viral way, both times it uses the codons CGG - the way that higher animals code for arginine.

claims.text: WIV stated in 2018 an intent to find viruses and add furin cleavage sites to them

attestations.verbatim_span: WIV, which said in 2018 that it was going to find viruses and add furin cleavage sites to them

claims.text: WIV found a close relative of BANAL-52 and added a furin cleavage site, using CGG because they were most familiar with human coding

attestations.verbatim_span: found a close relative of BANAL-52 and added a furin cleavage site. Since they were humans, and most familiar with the human way of encoding arginine, they added it as CGG both times.
attestations.framing: presented as 'the obvious conclusion'

claims.text: COVID was best at infecting human cells among fifty tested animal species

attestations.verbatim_span: Of fifty animals it was tested in, including the usual coronavirus intermediate hosts (pangolins, raccoon-dogs, etc), it was best at infecting human cells.
attestations.framing: COVID described as 'surprisingly optimized for infecting humans'

claims.text: Viruses entering a new species usually show a burst of mutations as they adapt

attestations.verbatim_span: a virus that enters a new species will usually show a burst of mutations as it “figures out” the best way to adapt to that species’ unique biology

claims.text: COVID's mutation rate was constant from the beginning to end of the pandemic, suggesting prior adaptation to humans

attestations.verbatim_span: COVID has had a pretty constant mutation rate in humans, from the beginning of the pandemic to the end. That suggests it was already adapted to humans.

claims.text: Research on dangerous coronaviruses is usually conducted at BSL-3 or BSL-4 security levels

attestations.verbatim_span: Usually, research with potentially dangerous coronaviruses is done in BSL-3 or 4, ie high to very-high security.

claims.text: WIV conducted this research at BSL-2, a medium security level

attestations.verbatim_span: But WIV was irresponsibly doing it in BSL-2, ie medium security.
attestations.framing: described as 'irresponsibly doing it'

claims.text: Researchers at WIV were not required to wear masks

attestations.verbatim_span: The researchers weren’t even required to wear masks.

claims.text: Approximately 1 in 500 labs will leak any given pathogen they work with

attestations.verbatim_span: In general, about 1/500 labs will leak any given pathogen they’re working on (?!).

claims.text: The odds of a leak were elevated because WIV researched an infectious virus irresponsibly

attestations.verbatim_span: But because WIV was researching such an infectious virus in such an irresponsible way, the odds of a leak were much higher.

claims.text: The most likely explanation is that WIV conducted the gain-of-function research they had proposed

attestations.verbatim_span: The most likely explanation for all these facts is that WIV went ahead and did the gain-of-function research they said they were going to do

claims.text: The DEFUSE grant proposal was rejected but demonstrates WIV's intent to do this research

attestations.verbatim_span: the particular DEFUSE grant proposal we know about got rejected, but it proves that Wuhan wanted to do this, and they could easily have gotten funding somewhere else, or done it out of their regular budget

claims.text: The virus leaked, spread in the general Wuhan population, and eventually reached the wet market

attestations.verbatim_span: Then it leaked, spread for a while in the general Wuhan population, and eventually made it to the wet market where it got detected.

claims.text: The DEFUSE grant proposal was rejected

attestations.verbatim_span: As mentioned earlier, the DEFUSE grant was rejected.

claims.text: The grant specified WIV would find viruses while UNC would perform gain-of-function research

attestations.verbatim_span: the grant said that the Wuhan Institute of Virology was responsible for finding the viruses, and the University of North Carolina would do all the gain-of-function research

claims.text: This division of labor was reasonable because UNC was skilled at gain-of-function research while WIV mostly was not

attestations.verbatim_span: This was a reasonable division of labor, since UNC was actually good at gain-of-function research, and WIV mostly wasn’t.

claims.text: WIV had only done a few simple gain-of-function projects previously and was not set up for this proposal

attestations.verbatim_span: They had done a few very simple gain-of-function projects before, but weren’t really set up for this particular proposal and were happy to leave it for their American colleagues.

claims.text: Even if WIV had attempted to create COVID, they lacked the necessary materials to do so

attestations.verbatim_span: Even if WIV did try to create COVID, they couldn’t have.

claims.text: WIV did not possess BANAL-52

attestations.verbatim_span: But WIV didn’t have BANAL-52.

claims.text: BANAL-52 was not discovered until after the pandemic began

attestations.verbatim_span: It wasn’t discovered until after the COVID pandemic started, when scientists scoured the area for potential COVID relatives.

claims.text: COVID could not have been created from RATG-13 because the two viruses are too different

attestations.verbatim_span: But you can’t create COVID from RATG-13; they’re too different.

claims.text: Creating COVID would require BANAL-52 or an as-yet-undiscovered extremely close relative, neither of which WIV had

attestations.verbatim_span: You would need BANAL-52, or some as-yet-undiscovered extremely close relative. WIV had neither.

claims.text: WIV regularly published lists of the coronaviruses they had discovered

attestations.verbatim_span: They published lists of which ones they had found pretty regularly.

claims.text: WIV's last published list was in mid-2019, shortly before the pandemic

attestations.verbatim_span: They published their last list in mid-2019, just a few months before the pandemic.

claims.text: Lab leak proponents claimed the virus lists showed discrepancies

attestations.verbatim_span: Although lab leak proponents claimed these lists showed weird discrepancies

claims.text: The apparent discrepancies were due to inconsistent naming rather than substantive differences

attestations.verbatim_span: this was just their inability to keep names consistent, and all the lists showed basically the same viruses

claims.text: The published lists included only RATG-13 among close COVID relatives, not BANAL-52 or similar

attestations.verbatim_span: The lists didn’t include BANAL-52 or any other suitable COVID relatives - only RATG-13, which isn’t close enough to work.

claims.text: WIV could not have kept discovery of BANAL-52 secret

attestations.verbatim_span: Could they have been keeping their discovery of BANAL-52 secret? No.

claims.text: Before the pandemic, BANAL-52 would not have appeared scientifically interesting enough to warrant secrecy

attestations.verbatim_span: Pre-pandemic, there was nothing interesting about it; our understanding of virology wasn’t good enough to point this out as a potential pandemic candidate.

claims.text: WIV conducted its gain-of-function research openly prior to the pandemic

attestations.verbatim_span: WIV did its gain-of-function research openly and proudly (before the pandemic, gain-of-function wasn’t as unpopular as it is now)

claims.text: COVID's furin cleavage site, while unusual, has characteristics consistent with natural rather than engineered origin

attestations.verbatim_span: COVID’s furin cleavage site is admittedly unusual. But it’s unusual in a way that looks natural rather than man-made.

claims.text: Labs typically add furin cleavage sites by mutating existing sequence rather than through nucleotide insertion

attestations.verbatim_span: Labs don’t usually add furin cleavage sites through nucleotide insertions (they usually mutate what’s already there).

claims.text: Viruses naturally acquire insertions of 12+ nucleotides

attestations.verbatim_span: viruses get weird insertions of 12+ nucleotides in nature

claims.text: HKU1, another emergent Chinese coronavirus, had a 15 nucleotide insertion near its furin cleavage site

attestations.verbatim_span: HKU1 is another emergent Chinese coronavirus that caused a small outbreak of pneumonia in 2004. It had a 15 nucleotide insertion right next to its furin cleavage site.

claims.text: Later strains of COVID acquired further 12-15 nucleotide insertions

attestations.verbatim_span: Later strains of COVID got further 12 - 15 nucleotide insertions.

claims.text: Many flu strains have 12-15 nucleotide insertions compared to earlier strains

attestations.verbatim_span: Plenty of flus have 12 to 15 nucleotide insertions compared to other earlier flu strains.

claims.text: Viral recombination with host RNA could explain unusual codon usage like CGG

attestations.verbatim_span: Other times the virus gets confused between its own RNA and its host’s, and splices a bit of the host RNA into the virus. This would neatly explain why the insertion used the unusual coding CGG for arginine, which is common in animals but rare in viruses.

claims.text: CGG codon usage in COVID overall is about 3%, indicating it is not extremely rare in viruses

attestations.verbatim_span: COVID uses CGG for arginine about 3% of the time.

claims.text: In a known example of human-engineered arginine insertion, none of the five arginines added used CGG

attestations.verbatim_span: Peter was able to find one example of humans adding arginine to a virus, and 0 out of the 5 arginines added were CGG.

claims.text: The standard human practice for engineered furin cleavage sites is RRKR

attestations.verbatim_span: When humans are inserting furin cleavage sites into viruses for gain-of-function, the standard practice is RRKR, a very nice and simple furin cleavage site which works well.

claims.text: COVID uses PRRAR, an unusual furin cleavage site never used by humans and expected to work poorly

attestations.verbatim_span: COVID uses PRRAR, a bizarre furin cleavage site which no human has ever used before, and which virologists expected to work poorly.
attestations.framing: described as 'bizarre'

claims.text: Later research found that an adjacent genomic region twists the protein in a way allowing PRRAR to function, but this was discovered only after COVID became significant

attestations.verbatim_span: They later found that an adjacent part of COVID’s genome twisted the protein in an unusual way that allowed PRRAR to be a viable furin cleavage site, but this discovery took a lot of computer power, and was only made after COVID became important.

claims.text: WIV researchers would not have known in advance that PRRAR would function

attestations.verbatim_span: The Wuhan virologists supposedly doing gain-of-function research on COVID shouldn’t have known this would work.

claims.text: COVID later mutated away from the PRRAR site toward a more standard form

attestations.verbatim_span: Even the virus eventually decided it worked better - sometime during the course of the pandemic, it mutated away from its weird PRRAR furin cleavage site towards a more normal form.

claims.text: The furin cleavage site was inserted via what appears to be a frameshift mutation rather than a clean insertion

attestations.verbatim_span: COVID’s furin cleavage site was inserted via what seems to be a frameshift mutation - it wasn’t a clean insertion of the amino acids that formed the site, it was an insertion of a sequence which changed the context of the surrounding nucleotides into the amino acids that formed the site.

claims.text: A frameshift-based insertion would be a pointless complexity with no reason for a human engineer to introduce, though consistent with random evolutionary processes

attestations.verbatim_span: This is a pointless too-clever-by-half “flourish” that there would be no reason for a human engineer to do. But it’s exactly the kind of weird thing that happens in the random chance of evolution.
attestations.framing: described as 'pointless too-clever-by-half flourish'

claims.text: COVID develops characteristic mutations when cultured in most standard media or animals

attestations.verbatim_span: If you culture it in most standard media or animals, it will quickly develop characteristic mutations.

claims.text: The original Wuhan strains lacked these characteristic culture-induced mutations

attestations.verbatim_span: But the original Wuhan strains didn’t have these mutations.

claims.text: COVID can only be cultured without such mutations in human airway cells or live raccoon-dogs

attestations.verbatim_span: The only ways to culture it without mutations are in human airway cells, or (apparently) in live raccoon-dogs.

claims.text: Human airway cell culturing requires a donor, and Wuhan had not previously used this technique

attestations.verbatim_span: Getting human airway cells requires a donor (ie someone who donates their body to science), and Wuhan had never done this before (it was one of the technologies only used at the superior North Carolina site).

claims.text: The virus's suitability for raccoon-dog culturing appears suspicious

attestations.verbatim_span: As for raccoon-dogs, it sure does seems suspicious that the virus is already suited to them.
attestations.framing: framed as 'suspicious'

claims.text: The claim that COVID is uniquely adapted to humans is false

attestations.verbatim_span: The claim that COVID is uniquely adapted to humans is false.

claims.text: The paper supporting human-adaptation defined adaptation by receptor similarity to humans, which biased humans to rank first by construction

attestations.verbatim_span: The paper that claimed that defined how well COVID was adapted to different animals by those animals’ difference (on the relevant cell receptors) from humans. So in its methodology, humans came out #1 by default.

claims.text: Using a different methodology, COVID appears better adapted to other animals than humans

attestations.verbatim_span: If you don’t do that, COVID is better-adapted to many other animals.

claims.text: It is not necessarily true that viruses show a mutation burst upon entering a new host species

attestations.verbatim_span: It’s not necessarily true that viruses see a burst of mutations when they enter a new host.

claims.text: COVID spread to deer and mink without showing a burst of mutations in either case

attestations.verbatim_span: COVID spread to deer and mink, and in neither case was there a burst of mutations.

claims.text: COVID is already well-suited to infecting respiratory cells regardless of host species

attestations.verbatim_span: COVID has a pretty simple job of infecting respiratory cells and is already very good at it, regardless of species.

claims.text: Yuri's model requires WIV to have picked up a discarded grant portion allotted to another university despite relative inexperience

attestations.verbatim_span: Wuhan Institute of Virology picked up a discarded grant and decided to do the gain-of-function half allotted to a different university, despite their relative inexperience.
attestations.framing: presented critically as a chain of implausible steps

claims.text: Yuri's model requires WIV to have skipped standard SARS-like viruses and model backbones in favor of BANAL-52

attestations.verbatim_span: They skipped over all the SARS-like viruses they were supposed to work on, and all the standard gain-of-function model backbones, in favor of BANAL-52
attestations.framing: presented critically

claims.text: Yuri's model requires WIV to have possessed BANAL-52 samples two years before its official discovery and kept them secret despite no apparent reason

attestations.verbatim_span: a virus which would not be discovered for another two years, but which they somehow had samples of, which they had for some reason decided to keep secret despite its total lack of interestingness
attestations.framing: presented critically as implausible

claims.text: Yuri's model requires WIV to have abandoned usual gain-of-function practices for an unusual furin cleavage site that shouldn't have worked per contemporary theory, inserted via frameshift

attestations.verbatim_span: Then they would have had to eschew all usual gain-of-function practices in favor of inserting a weird furin cleavage site that shouldn’t have worked according to the theory they had at the time, via a frameshift mutation.
attestations.framing: presented critically as implausible

claims.text: Yuri's model requires WIV to have cultured the virus despite this being beyond their capabilities, and the virus to leak and reappear at a wet market raccoon-dog stall

attestations.verbatim_span: Then they would have had to culture it, a technique beyond their limited capabilities. Then it would have had to leak, and magically show up again in front of the raccoon-dog stall at a wet market.
attestations.framing: described as needing to 'magically show up'

claims.text: WIV would not need to keep BANAL-52 secret in a sinister way, since researchers commonly have unpublished backlogs

attestations.verbatim_span: WIV wouldn’t have needed to keep BANAL-52 “secret” in some kind of sinister way. Plenty of researchers have backlogs of work they haven’t published yet.

claims.text: WIV likely found a BANAL relative during normal sampling trips and delayed publication while cleaning data

claims.text: Hypothesis testing involves computing a test statistic, normalizing it to a p-value, and rejecting the null hypothesis if the p-value is below a chosen threshold

attestations.verbatim_span: the hypothesis testing method involves three steps: Compute a test statistic , which is a random variable that is a function of some collection of observations. Normalize the test statistic so that it is uniformly distributed in the range [0, 1]. This normalized value is called the p-value. Choose a desired false negative rate, and “reject” the null hypothesis if the p-value is below this threshold.

attestations.verbatim_span: If instead we observe 70 heads out of 100, we would have gotten a p-value of 0.99997, and we would fail to reject the null hypothesis even though we know intuitively that the observation contains enough information to do so

claims.text: A better normalization sorts possible observations by how informative they are in rejecting the null hypothesis, giving 0 or 100 heads the lowest p-values

attestations.verbatim_span: Once you accept that, it is hard to imagine what is the most probable explanation under the lab-leak theory for how the first cluster arose at HSM other than coincidence, which I put at about 1 in 5000 (after accounting for potential biases in viral transmission that might favor the virus spreading at HSM).

claims.text: The most probable non-coincidence explanation is likely an unknown unknown the author has not been able to think of

claims.text: It is fair to partially include unknown unknowns at the end after including all factors, which dampens the final number toward 1/2 but cannot make it cross that line

attestations.verbatim_span: I think it is fair to at least partially include unknown unknowns at the end , after including all factors, as some unknown unknowns (eg: if I am overestimating my ability to assess the data) will be entangled with multiple observations in unpredictable ways. This dampens the final number towards 1/2, as I stated in the report, but cannot make it cross the 1/2 line.

claims.text: It is fair to modestly reduce the 1 in 5000 estimate to account for uncertainty, and the author already gave it a modest reduction

attestations.verbatim_span: I do think it is fair to modestly reduce the 1 in 5000 number to account for uncertainty, but as I said in the report, it is the only Bayes factor that actually comes from calculating something (as opposed to me making up a number that feels good), and I already gave it a modest reduction.

attestations.framing: opposition to public health measures is described as "cruel"

claims.text: The author intends to objectively calculate the odds that SC2 came from a lab.

attestations.verbatim_span: In what follows I will try to objectively calculate the odds that SC2 came from a lab, in the hope that will be useful to anyone thinking about future research policy.

claims.text: The method used is robust Bayesian analysis, which updates beliefs systematically without overweighting priors or new evidence.

attestations.verbatim_span: Our method will be robust Bayesian analysis, a systematic way of updating beliefs without putting too much weight on either one’s prior beliefs or the new evidence.

claims.text: Bayesian analysis does not require any single piece of evidence to be dispositive or to fit rigid qualitative categories.

attestations.verbatim_span: Bayesian analysis does not insist that any single piece of evidence be “dispositive” or fit into any rigid qualitative verbal category.

claims.text: No hypothesis is granted categorical qualitative superiority as a null hypothesis with a distinct burden of proof under this method.

attestations.verbatim_span: Some hypotheses can start with a big subjective head-start, but none are granted categorical qualitative superiority as “null hypotheses” and none have to carry a qualitatively distinct “burden of proof”.

claims.text: Each piece of evidence receives quantitative weight based on its consistency with competing hypotheses.

attestations.verbatim_span: Each piece of evidence gets some quantitative weight based on its consistency with competing hypotheses.

claims.text: Robust Bayesian analysis provides a systematic way to account for subjective uncertainties in prior probabilities and evidence weights.

attestations.verbatim_span: “ Robust Bayesian analysis “ provides a systematic way of taking those uncertainties about the evidence into account.

claims.text: The author is aware of six other more-or-less published Bayesian analyses of SC2 origins.

attestations.verbatim_span: I am aware of six more-or-less published Bayesian analyses of SC2 origins, other than my own very preliminary inconclusive one.

claims.text: The four comprehensive prior Bayesian analyses reach conclusions similar to the one presented here.

attestations.verbatim_span: The four that attempt to be comprehensive come to conclusions similar to the one I shall reach here.

claims.text: The analysis will compare the probability of wildlife origin to the probability of origin via work similar to that described in the 2018 DEFUSE grant proposal.

attestations.verbatim_span: I will focus on comparing the probability that SC2 originated in wildlife vs. the probability that it originated in work similar to that described in the 2018 DEFUSE grant proposal

attestations.verbatim_span: the 2018 DEFUSE grant proposal submitted to the US Defense Advanced Research Project Agency from institutions that included the University of North Carolina (UNC), the National University of Singapore, and the EcoHealth Alliance as well as the Wuhan Institute of Virology (WIV)

claims.text: DEFUSE was not funded by DARPA.

attestations.verbatim_span: Although DEFUSE was not funded by DARPA

attestations.verbatim_span: anyone who has run a grant-supported research lab knows that work on yet-to-be-funded projects routinely continues except when it requires major new expenses such as purchasing large equipment items

attestations.verbatim_span: I will not discuss any claims about bioweapons research.

attestations.verbatim_span: It is not exactly likely that a secret military project would request funding from DARPA for work shared between UNC and WIV.

claims.text: The author will not use rumors of roadblocks around WIV, cell-phone gaps, sick researchers, or disappearances because they cannot be reliably sorted from politically motivated reports.

attestations.verbatim_span: My analysis will not make use of the rumors of roadblocks around WIV, cell-phone use gaps, sick WIV researchers, disappearances of researchers, etc. That sort of evidence might someday be important but at this point I can’t sort it out from the haze of politically motivated reports.

claims.text: Executive summaries from various agencies on origins are inconclusive and of limited use.
